activities_projects.title: Quantitative Validation of Mass Conservation
activities_projects.objective: To experimentally verify that the total mass of reactants equals the total mass of products in a chemical reaction.
materials: Solutions of Copper sulphate and Sodium carbonate
steps: Prepare 5% solutions of the chosen pair of chemicals separately.
Place sodium carbonate solution in the flask and copper sulphate in the ignition tube.
Suspend the tube in the flask using a thread and seal with a cork.
Weigh the entire assembly accurately.
Tilt and swirl the flask to mix the solutions and observe the chemical change.
Reweigh the flask and compare the results.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Demonstrates the Law of Conservation of Mass, proving mass is neither created nor destroyed during chemical transformations.
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why a cork is necessary for the accuracy of this experiment (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Scale Modeling of Atomic Radii
activities_projects.objective: To visualize and compare the nanometric scale of atoms and molecules relative to macroscopic objects.
steps: Review the unit conversion: $1/10^{9}$ m = 1 nm.
Draw a line representing 1 meter on chart paper.
Mark the relative positions of an ant ($10^{-3}$ m), a grain of sand ($10^{-4}$ m), and an atom of hydrogen ($10^{-10}$ m) using a logarithmic scale.
Stack several sheets of paper and discuss how many millions of atoms would be required to reach that thickness.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Develops a conceptual understanding of the scale of atomic dimensions compared to the macroscopic world.
activities_projects.evaluation: Convert the radius of a water molecule ($10^{-9}$ m) into nanometers (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Decoding Chemical Symbols and Origins
activities_projects.objective: To identify elements by their symbols and understand their etymological origins.
materials: Table of symbols for some elements
steps: Identify symbols derived from English names (e.g., H for Hydrogen, Al for Aluminium).
Identify symbols derived from Latin, Greek, or German (e.g., Fe from ferrum, Na from natrium).
Practice writing symbols following IUPAC rules: first letter capital, second letter small.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Mastery of chemical shorthand and the standardized naming conventions of elements.
activities_projects.evaluation: Write the Latin names and symbols for Iron, Sodium, and Potassium (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Ratio by Mass in Compounds
activities_projects.objective: To verify the Law of Constant Proportions through mathematical analysis of compound compositions.
materials: Reference Table of ratios by mass
steps: Analyze the mass ratio of Hydrogen to Oxygen in water ($1:8$).
Calculate the mass of oxygen required to react with a specific mass of hydrogen (e.g., 3 g).
Repeat the calculation for Ammonia ($NH_{3}$) using the ratio $14:3$.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that elements are always present in definite proportions by mass in a chemical substance.
activities_projects.evaluation: Determine the mass of oxygen needed to react with 3 g of hydrogen to form water (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Valency-Based Formula Construction
activities_projects.objective: To use the 'criss-cross' method and combining capacity (valency) to write chemical formulae.
materials: Table of names and symbols of some ions
steps: Identify the valency or charge for given ions (e.g., $Mg^{2+}$, $Cl^{-}$).
Write the symbols of the cation first, followed by the anion.
Criss-cross the valencies to determine the subscripts in the formula.
Simplify ratios where necessary (e.g., $Ca_{2}O_{2}$ becomes $CaO$).
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Ability to predict and write the symbolic representation of a compound's composition.
activities_projects.evaluation: Write the chemical formulae for Aluminium oxide and Magnesium chloride (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Molecules vs. Ions: Classification
activities_projects.objective: To differentiate between independent molecular units and charged ionic clusters.
materials: List of substances: $Ar, O_{2}, NaCl, NaOH, (NH_{4})_{2}SO_{4}$
steps: Classify substances as monoatomic, diatomic, or polyatomic.
Identify 'anions' and 'cations' in ionic compounds.
Recognize polyatomic ions (groups of atoms with a net charge).
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Distinguishes between different ways atoms aggregate to form matter.
activities_projects.evaluation: Define 'atomicity' and list the atomicity of Argon and Oxygen (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Calculating Formula Unit and Molecular Mass
activities_projects.objective: To determine the relative mass of a substance by summing atomic masses.
materials: Table of atomic masses of a few elements
steps: Identify the atomic mass for each element in the compound.
For molecular substances ($H_{2}O$), sum the atomic masses of all atoms in the molecule.
For ionic substances ($CaCl_{2}$), calculate the formula unit mass using the same summation method.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Applies the concept of relative atomic mass to find the total mass of molecules and ionic units.
activities_projects.evaluation: Calculate the molecular mass of $HNO_{3}$ (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Analyzing Postulates of Dalton's Theory
activities_projects.objective: To link Dalton's atomic theory postulates to established laws of chemical combination.
steps: Read the postulate regarding indivisible atoms and link it to the Law of Conservation of Mass.
Read the postulate regarding constant relative numbers of atoms and link it to the Law of Definite Proportions.
Discuss how the postulate of elements combining in simple ratios explains compound formation.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the theoretical framework that provides the basis for chemical science laws.
activities_projects.evaluation: Which postulate of Dalton's theory is a result of the Law of Conservation of Mass? (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Polyatomic Ion Model Building
activities_projects.objective: To visualize clusters of atoms acting as single charged units.
steps: Model a single ion (e.g., $Na^{+}$) as a single unit.
Model a polyatomic ion (e.g., $SO_{4}^{2-}$) as a cluster that moves together.
Demonstrate how two sodium units fix onto one sulphate unit to balance the charge.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Clarifies the behavior of polyatomic ions in chemical combinations and formula writing.
activities_projects.evaluation: Define polyatomic ions and give two examples (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Predicting Ionic Compound Ratios
activities_projects.objective: To use mass ratios to find the simplest numerical ratio of atoms in a molecule.
steps: Note the mass ratio for water (H:O = 1:8).
Divide mass ratios by atomic masses ($1/1$ and $8/16$).
Find the simplest whole-number ratio to derive the H:O = 2:1 atomic ratio.
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Bridges the gap between macroscopic mass measurements and microscopic atomic counts.
activities_projects.evaluation: Show the calculation to find the atomic ratio for a water molecule (10 marks).
